data.title: Gold rises on US-Iran tensions and lower European bond yields
data.description:
Key points:
  • US sets 15-day deadline for Iran to sign nuclear deal
  • Silver, platinum and palladium set for weekly gains
  • US inflation data due at 1330 GMT

By Pablo Sinha

Gold prices rose on Friday, supported by lingering tensions between the United States and Iran and lower European bond yields while investors awaited U.S. inflation data to assess the outlook for Federal Reserve monetary policy.

Spot gold was up 0.7% at $5,032.49 an ounce by 0941 GMT, but was on course for a weekly decline of 0.2%. U.S. gold futures for April delivery were up 1.1% at $5,052.70.

"What we're seeing in the interest rate markets is that prices are higher in the longer end in European trading. That means yields are down, and that is reducing opportunity costs for holding gold," said Quantitative Commodity Research analyst Peter Fertig.

Euro zone government bond yields were set for a second straight weekly decline on Friday in a week marked by speculation over the leadership of the European Central Bank and by rising geopolitical tensions.

U.S. President Donald Trump warned Iran on Thursday that it must make a deal over its nuclear programme or "really bad things" will happen, setting a deadline of 10 to 15 days.

Goldman Sachs said in a note that in its base case, with central bank buying re-accelerating and private investors adding exposure only in response to U.S. interest rate cuts, it expects gold to grind slowly higher this year.

U.S. personal consumption expenditure data, the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge, is in focus for clues on U.S. monetary policy. Economists polled by Reuters forecast a 0.3% rise in core PCE. The data is due at 1330 GMT.

Traders largely expect the central bank to hold interest rates steady at its March policy meeting, according to CME's FedWatch Tool.

Non-yielding bullion tends to perform well when interest rates are low.

Gold demand remained subdued in India this week as volatile prices deterred buyers while other major Asian hubs, including China, were closed for the Lunar New Year holidays.

Elsewhere, spot silver gained 2.8% to $80.57 an ounce. Spot platinum rose 2.3% to $2,117.66 while palladium added 2.3% to $1,723.25. All metals were poised to register weekly gains.

data.title: Rule changes boost retail bond market access, says NSDL CEO
data.description:
India’s retail bond market is finally expanding, and according to Vijay Chandok, MD & CEO of National Securities Depository Limited (NSDL), the real trigger is not investor awareness but regulation that made bonds investable.

Explaining what changed structurally, Chandok said the turning point was the introduction of Online Bond Platform Provider (OBPP) rules. On the key catalyst, he said, “The real, compelling regulation… which has really propelled the bond market forward is the OBPP regulations.” The framework has effectively opened access to bonds, much as online platforms earlier widened participation in equities and mutual funds.

The impact is already visible in distribution.

On platform growth, Chandok stated, “We have more than 40 of them in the country now,” adding that NSDL has partnered with many of these platforms to provide digital onboarding and simplified processes for investors. He said the industry has moved toward “facilitation, paperlessness, digitalisation, information flow,” which is pushing retail participation higher.

Another major hurdle earlier was the lack of transparency and investor understanding. NSDL is attempting to solve this through information access.

On investor education, Chandok said the depository provides “a plethora of information about bonds… which really helps a retail investor to understand the risks and then take an informed decision,” including yield and pricing data through its trade repository.

Lower entry barriers are also bringing bonds closer to a mass retail product. Chandok pointed to SEBI’s recent policy move.

On accessibility, he said, “SEBI has brought down the minimum investment from ₹100,000 to ₹10,000. I think that's a very positive step.” Along with growing advisor participation, bonds are increasingly being positioned as a diversification tool for retail portfolios.

He believes the market is now beginning to follow the adoption path once seen in mutual funds — digital platforms, advisors, and smaller-ticket sizes. “We are beginning to see some components of this coming to the bond market as well,” he said, adding that a conducive regulatory and interest-rate environment is supporting participation.

Despite the progress, India’s bond market remains small compared to bank financing and global peers, though it is steadily expanding.

On issuance growth, Chandok said, “Two years back, we saw the total issuances of about nine lakh crore… this year it's likely to be eleven.” He stressed that further expansion will depend on investor education, stating bond concepts are not easy for first-time investors and “explanation is important” for wider adoption.

Catch all the latest updates from the stock market here

data.title: 'Borderline hoarder': Tori Spelling says her $12,000-a-month rental where she lives with her 5 kids is a mess
data.description:
By Kelsi Karruli

Her podcast co-host criticizes Spelling's lifestyle.

Tori Spelling attends an iHeartRadio event in Los Angeles Dec. 5, 2025.

TV personality Tori Spelling has admitted that she is living like a "borderline hoarder," confessing that she no longer feels comfortable having people visit her lavish California rental home because it has fallen into such a state of disarray.

The 52-year-old actress opened up about the falling standards at her property-which is understood to be located in Calabasas and costs Spelling around $12,000 a month-during a new appearance on her iHeartRadio podcast, "misSpelling."

Returning to the show after a two-month hiatus, the "Beverly Hills, 90210" star explained that she had taken time off to deal with a spate of illnesses, revealing that one particularly bad bout had left her unable to work for eight weeks.

"I know I have had a past of getting sick a lot and frequently, but this one took me down eight weeks. I couldn't work," she said.

Spelling blamed her five children for the outbreak at her property, joking: "I mean it started with the five petri dishes that live in my house, but they bring things home, that's normal. And, you know, you work through it, you get sick."

However, her co-host, Amy Sugarman, claimed there may be another factor to blame for her ongoing health issues.

"[Your] lifestyle contributes to why you get sick so much," she said, hinting that Spelling's surroundings may also be playing a role in her illnesses.

To prove that point, Sugarman asked Spelling to send her a photo of the current state of her home, then exclaiming, "Oh, it's not good," when she saw the image.

"I don't want to be mean, but ... the Christmas tree is up," Sugarman continued, looking at the snap.

"Damn," Spelling replied, "I thought I cut that out!"

Her co-host, however, refused to back down on her critique of the state of Spelling's home, insisting that there is plenty of potential within the dwelling but that it is hard to see for all the mess.

"You can see the bones in here, like the shelves could be really pretty; the living room could be really pretty. You have beautiful things. You can't even sit in there," she said.

"It's borderline hoarder, I'm not going to lie," Sugarman added, as Spelling was forced to agree.

Tori Spelling in 2024.

The barrage didn't end there, with the co-host telling Spelling that while her own home looks like "two sacks of s-t," the Hollywood heiress' property looks like it has "400 sacks of s-t in [it] right now."

Despite agreeing with much of Sugarman's comments, Spelling did attempt to defend herself, noting that, while her living room may be a mess, the kitchen-which is where everyone typically "hangs and eats"-is clean.

Still, Spelling appeared somewhat mortified by the state of her property, admitting that she has even stopped having guests over to the property because it's so messy, noting that the policy even applies to the iHeart Radio crews who record her show.

When asked by Sugarman whether she would consent to hosting the president of the radio network, Spelling responded: "I would have to pass. I'd be like, 'Where? I'll come to you wherever you are.' I would change everything. I stopped having people over."

More from Realtor.com: Phantom Humming Plagues Connecticut Town-Drawing Hundreds of Complaints

The A-lister noted that her home wasn't always a mess, explaining that when she was with her ex-husband, Dean McDermott, he would keep their dwelling clean.

"When Dean and I were together and lived together, I was able to have a house that was presentable because he wouldn't live like that. We were working at different times, so I had downtime when he'd work," she said.

Spelling further insisted that if someone helped her organize it, she would be able to maintain it.

"My busy, chaotic work and children lifestyle is not going to change. In fact, in the next year, there's going to be things coming up, it's going to be escalated. So I need to be on my A-game, and once someone comes in and does all this, yes, I can maintain it if I have someone helping me," she said.

More from Realtor.com: Kelly Clarkson Reveals She's Ending Her NBC Talk Show To 'Prioritize' Her Kids-so, Will the TV Host Leave New York for Good?

Spelling previously questioned if she was a "hoarder" during a candid conversation with Robin Zasio, psychologist from the show "Hoarders."

She also revealed she had 50 storage units the size of "shipping containers" as well as a bill of $80,000. She ultimately had to sell the items inside to pay the bill.

More from Realtor.com: Done Waiting: Citizens Push To Abolish Property Taxes, State by State

Spelling reportedly resides in a $12,000-a-month rental in Calabasas with her five children: Liam, 18, Stella, 17, Hattie, 14, Finn, 13, and Beau, 8.

In 2023, Spelling made headlines when it was revealed that she lived in an RV following a severe mold issue in her previous rental, which caused chronic illnesses.

Spelling is most famous for her role as Donna Martin on "Beverly Hills, 90210," a TV series produced by her father, Aaron Spelling. She's also appeared in "Scary Movie 2," "Trick," and "House of Yes."

The actress was raised in a spectacular Holmby Hills estate that was custom-built for her parents and became widely known as Spelling Manor.

More from Realtor.com: Trump Releases First Rendering of the Planned White House Ballroom

Aaron and his wife, Candy Spelling, built the sprawling estate in 1990 and lived there for more than 20 years. After Aaron died, Candy placed the home on the market for the sky-high price of $150 million. It was eventually sold for $85 million in 2011 to Formula 1 heiress Petra Ecclestone.

This story originally ran on Realtor.com.

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: EPAM Systems Is Maintained at Buy by Needham
data.description: (10:08 GMT) EPAM Systems Price Target Cut to $175.00/Share From $215.00 by Needham

data.title: Slovenia Unemployment Rate at 10-Month High
data.description:
Slovenia’s unemployment rate rose to 4.8% in December 2025 from 4.6% in the previous two months, marking the highest level since February.

The number of unemployed persons increased by 1,381 month-on-month to 47,177.

Unemployed men totaled 24,574, while women accounted for 22,603.

By age group, unemployment stood at 7.7% for those aged 15–29, 4.8% for those aged 50 and older, and 5.8% for those aged 55 and above.

For comparison, the jobless rate was 4.8% in December of the previous year.

Meanwhile, employers reported 9,489 job vacancies, down 3.3% year-on-year, and 157,803 vacancies for the full year, up slightly from 157,384 in the same period last year.

data.title: Anglo American hit by De Beers writedown, posts $3.7 billion loss
data.description:
Key points:
  • Mining group advancing plans to shed non-core assets
  • Company declares dividend of $0.23 a share
  • Core earnings of $6.4 billion in line with analysts' estimate

By Clara Denina

Anglo American posted a $3.7 billion loss on Friday after taking another writedown on its diamonds business, as the miner pushes ahead with plans to shed non-core assets and complete its merger with Teck Resources .

Anglo wrapped up a mixed reporting season for London-listed mining groups, underscoring the industry's diverging fortunes as Antofagasta benefited from soaring copper prices while diversified peers struggled with weaker iron ore, diamond and coal markets.

The company booked a $2.3 billion pre-tax impairment tied to its De Beers unit, reducing the carrying value to $2.3 billion from more than $4 billion previously.

Core earnings, or EBITDA, of $6.4 billion were in line with an analysts' estimate. It declared a dividend of $0.23 per share, or about $200 million. That was down from $0.64 a share, or $800 million, a year earlier.

The company's share price was up 1.7% by 0919 GMT.

Anglo, which in July discontinued nickel and steelmaking coal assets that it is seeking to sell, aims to focus on copper and iron ore assets.

It demerged its platinum business in May, and said it is moving forward with plans to sell De Beers.

The company announced a potential partnership with Mitsubishi Corp for its Woodsmith fertiliser project in northern England, which it had placed on care and maintenance.

"We believe this potential partnership would add optionality and time to pursue further syndication/partnerships," said Goldman Sachs analysts.

DE BEERS SPIN-OFF

Anglo has reassessed the value of De Beers after the diamonds unit posted a third consecutive annual drop in production. It also cut De Beers' 2026 production forecast as weak demand and high inventories continue to weigh on the diamond market.

Anglo had already written down De Beers' value by about $3.5 billion over the past two years. "There is at the moment a plentiful supply of rough diamonds in the market," CEO Duncan Wanblad told reporters.

The sale of De Beers is at an advanced stage, he said. "We need to... get to final binding bids and then pick the partner that we want to go forward with and negotiate that with all the parties involved, including the government of Botswana," he added.

De Beers, which Anglo put up for sale as part of a broader restructuring, has drawn interest from multiple consortia, Wanblad said.

Botswana, already a 15% shareholder and source of 70% of its annual rough diamond production, has said it intends to increase its stake.

Angola is pursuing a 20-30% stake in De Beers, a proposal under discussion with other African diamond producers, a senior official from Angola's mining ministry told Reuters earlier this month.

Wanblad said he is "optimistic" that a deal will be signed this year.

TECK TIE-UP

As large-scale mining consolidation remains elusive despite pressure on companies to expand their copper portfolios, Anglo is the only major miner to have secured a deal, announcing in September a $53 billion all-stock, nil-premium merger with Teck.

On Friday, Wanblad said he expects the deal to be approved some time between September and March, as it awaits China and South Korea's regulatory approvals.

The deal, which would create the world's fifth-largest copper producer, proceeded even as the world's largest miner BHP Group attempted to take over Anglo.

The combined entity is projected to produce more than 1.2 million metric tons of copper annually.

Copper, a key metal for the power and construction industries, is poised to benefit from surging demand driven by electric vehicles and artificial intelligence.

("ZIOC" or the "Company")

Recording of Investor Presentation

Zanaga Iron Ore Company Limited (AIM: ZIOC) is pleased to announce that a recording of the investor presentation, delivered by the Company during Mining Indaba 2026, is now available to view here: https://www.zanagairon.com/media/

The presentation, hosted by Martin Knauth, CEO, and Andrew Trahar, Corporate Development & Investor Relations Manager, covers the proposed strategic investment by Red Arc Minerals, announced on 10 February 2026, and the subsequent development plan for the Zanaga Project.

For further information, please contact:

About ZIOC:

Zanaga Iron Ore Company Limited (AIM ticker: ZIOC) is an iron ore exploration and development company, with its flagship asset being the 100% owned Zanaga Iron Ore Project, located in the Republic of Congo. The Government Mining Licence, Environmental Permit and Mining Convention are all in place for the Project.

The Zanaga Iron Ore Project is a globally significant asset with a 6.9 billion tonne resource and a 2.1 billion tonne reserve, targeting 30Mtpa production of high-grade DRI pellet feed with very low impurity levels. When fully developed, Stage One (12Mtpa) and Stage Two (18Mtpa expansion) together could establish Zanaga as one of the world's largest iron ore mines. With all key permits secured, Zanaga is well positioned to benefit from increasing demand for high-quality, low-impurity iron ore, supported by low operating costs and an efficient slurry pipeline to port.

In the context of the global transition towards lower-carbon steel production, the Zanaga Project is well positioned to become one of the largest producers of high-grade, premium DRI pellet feed iron ore concentrate.

The Zanaga Iron Ore Company Limited LEI number is 21380085XNXEX6NL6L23.

data.title: European shares set for weekly gains as earnings outlook improves; geopolitical tensions in focus
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 index nears all-time high, driven by luxury sector gains
  • AI-disruption concerns ease, boosting banks and corporate earnings outlook
  • Unipol reports 36.8% profit increase, boosting shares
  • Defence stocks rise nearly 4% amid Middle East tensions

By Johann M Cherian

European shares edged higher on Friday and were on track for weekly gains, buoyed by a broadly improving corporate earnings outlook and easing AI-disruption worries, while simmering geopolitical tensions kept a lid on enthusiasm.

The pan-European STOXX 600 index rose 0.48% to 628.35 points by 0942 GMT, trading just shy of an all-time high, with most regional benchmarks also in the black.

Luxury brand Moncler <MONC.MI> jumped 11.3% after reporting a 7% rise in revenue in the fourth quarter, helped by solid growth in Asia and the Americas.

The personal and household goods sector (.SXQP) added 1.4%, while gains spilled over to the broader luxury sector (.STXLUXP) that rose 2%.

The STOXX index was also set to mark its biggest weekly jump since early January as investors were relieved by an overall improving corporate earnings outlook, while concerns that new artificial intelligence models could imminently disrupt traditional businesses temporarily took a back seat.

Anis Lahlou, chief investment officer of European equities at Aperture Investors, said the big wake-up call was discovering software, which was thought to be the best return on investment, was no longer all of a sudden.

"An important question of stock picking now is: what is the risk of disruption from AI on this business model? - this is not the usual question you ask when you were looking at stocks before. But now this is the first question you want to ask when you open a meeting with a company."

Banks (.SX7E), (.SX7P), which had witnessed sharp losses at the peak of the AI-disruption selloff, were among the top sectoral performers this week. However, those concerns are likely to resurface as investors scrutinize ways the technology could affect businesses.

Meanwhile, global markets were monitoring developments in the oil-rich Middle East as President Donald Trump warned Iran it must make a deal over its nuclear program or "really bad things" will happen. Defence stocks (.SXPARO) were marginally higher and has jumped nearly 4% this week.

The focus later in the day will shift to initial business activity surveys out of eurozone economies, along with a key inflation report out of the U.S.

Among others, life sciences firm Siegfried slid 6.7% after its annual revenue slightly missed analyst expectations.

Italian insurer Unipol <UNPI.MI> gained 6% after reporting a 36.8% rise in its full-year profit. Companies that reported results on Friday also included French food group Danone <DANO.PA> and UK's Anglo American .

data.title: Peru's markets largely unfazed as its revolving-door presidency spins again
data.description:
Key points:
  • Peru's bonds stable despite political upheaval
  • Balcazar pledges continuity, vows no macroeconomic experiments
  • April election has record number of candidates

By Lucinda Elliott and Leila Miller

Peru’s revolving-door presidency turned again this week, ushering out the country's seventh leader in less than a decade, but the political churn has produced little market turbulence as investors seem unfazed by the spin.

In an unexpected move ahead of elections on April 12, Peru's Congress voted to oust President Jose Jeri on Tuesday over a corruption scandal only four months into his term.

The body on Wednesday elected hard‑left lawmaker Jose Balcazar as interim president to serve until the next president takes office on July 28.

In a region where political upheaval often rattles markets, Peru is an outlier. This week's turbulence has caused few ripples in the markets as the country again demonstrates resilience in the face of a crisis that would trigger sharp selloffs in other economies.

“Portfolio investors have become desensitized to Peru’s political drama,” said Eileen Gavin, head of sovereign analysis at Verisk Maplecroft. "The mining-focused economy’s macro and credit risk metrics have remained fairly exemplary throughout a near decade of instability.”

Investors still view Peru as one of the safest credit markets in the region. It is among the countries closely trailing Chile for the narrowest spreads between yields on its benchmark government debt and U.S. Treasuries, a sign that investors perceive the country as relatively low risk.

Peru’s 2060 sovereign bond dipped slightly on Thursday, changing hands at around 56 cents on the dollar, emerging‑market debt analysts said. The country's 2032 dollar-denominated notes held steady, Refinitiv data showed.

After he was sworn in on Wednesday, Balcazar pledged he would oversee “unquestionable” elections in the world’s No. 2 copper exporter and vowed to avoid "experimenting" with economic policy — a note of continuity aimed squarely at investors.

Analysts said Peru’s commodity-driven economy and confidence in its long-serving central bank governor have historically insulated markets from political turbulence.

The combination of a copper-dependent economy and a highly credible central bank has cushioned Peru from politics before, said Jo-Marie Burt, a Peru specialist at George Mason University.

FRAGMENTED RACE

But 2026 will bring another significant test of Peru’s electoral institutions, and analysts said Balcazar’s unexpected ascent could complicate a fragmented race.

Balcazar, 83, has a controversial past. Before he entered Congress in 2021, he was dismissed as a provisional Supreme Court justice for misconduct then disbarred.

In 2023, he was skewered for comments widely viewed as defending child marriage, an episode revived by local media this week.

Moreover, a record number of presidential candidates is running in April and voters will also elect a new 60-seat Senate in a restored bicameral Congress.

“I see a lack of time for voters to coalesce around a good candidate,” said Cynthia McClintock of George Washington University.

“There’s already a feeling of, 'There are so many candidates — how are we supposed to cope?’” she added.

About 42% of respondents in an Ipsos poll this month said they were either undecided, planned to void their vote or intended to leave the ballot blank.

McClintock said the continued turnover in the presidency resulted from Congress asserting its removal powers too freely, a tactic that has disillusioned voters.

Jeri replaced Dina Boluarte, who stood in for leftist President Pedro Castillo following his impeachment after a rocky tenure of just 17 months.

Friction among political parties in Congress could spill into the upcoming presidential vote, where Rafael Lopez Aliaga, the right‑wing Lima mayor, leads most polls but may struggle to win a likely runoff without broader conservative support.

Behind him is Keiko Fujimori, the daughter of Peru's late former President Alberto Fujimori.

Still, if the elections are seen as credible, "the market will likely keep looking past the politics," Gavin said.

data.title: Bitcoin Bottom Call On Ice: Fear Is Extreme, Whales Aren’t Buying
data.description:
Bitcoin’s slide into the $60,000–$70,000 zone has lit up the usual “bottom” dashboards: extreme fear, washed-out positioning, and a cluster of indicators many traders treat as capitulation signals. But CryptoQuant contributor Mignolet says the market is missing the only thing that ultimately matters: a visible bid from dominant buyers.

“What I emphasized in the $80K–$90K range still remains the same,” he wrote on Feb. 18. “Many indicators that market participants follow are pointing to a bottom and extreme fear. However, we do not see dominant players (whales) actually using this situation.”

Mignolet’s core argument is simple: a bottom is not a sentiment reading, it’s an event and he doesn’t see the kind of forced absorption that typically marks a durable turn. “No matter how many indicators suggest a bottom, if there is no real buying force stepping in, we cannot know where the true bottom will be,” he said. “That is why I do not make price predictions lightly.”

He contrasted the current tape with the 2024 bull cycle, when fear could still dominate headlines even as large allocators quietly took the other side. In that period, he argues, the market had a measurable backstop: institutional demand showing up through US spot Bitcoin ETFs, specifically BlackRock’s IBIT and Fidelity’s FBTC, which “clearly absorbed the selling pressure.”

The “most important point,” in his framing, is that the same mechanics aren’t showing up now. Mignolet says the accumulation pattern FBTC sustained for roughly a year has “already broken down,” and IBIT, previously described as a buffer during heavy sell pressure, is “now trending downward, unlike last year.”

That shift is why he keeps the bottom call “on ice,” even if price ultimately holds the current region. In his view, Bitcoin remains in a phase where traders should “be cautious about further shocks,” and even a successful defense would likely require time before it can be treated as confirmed.

When Everyone Reads The Same Bitcoin Data

Beyond flow, Mignolet is also warning about a structural change in how market narratives form. He argues the proliferation of on-chain analytics has made the space more information-dense, but not necessarily more insightful and in some cases, more hazardous.

“The problem is that everyone looks at the same data and often reaches similar conclusions,” he wrote. “In many cases, even the people producing the data do not fully understand it. When information becomes too common, it pushes expectations in one direction.”

He describes today’s well-packaged on-chain dashboards as “clean and convincing, almost like an answer sheet,” which can harden conviction precisely when flexibility is required. The downstream risk, he suggests, is that widespread agreement around “obvious” bottoms can keep investors anchored through deeper drawdowns or longer grind periods.

In the near term, Mignolet’s base case is not a clean trend reversal but “sideways movement without a clear direction,” with enough volatility to create opportunities for short-term traders. For his own positioning, he described the period as “waiting,” stepping back to watch “liquidity flows, supply and demand conditions, and overall market sentiment,” then “reset” his framework.

The bigger picture, he says, is still bearish and potentially more drawn out than he expected last year. His closing warning is that this down cycle is “unlikely to end lightly,” with the plausible outcomes being a larger-than-expected drop, a longer-than-expected sideways phase, or both.

At press time, Bitcoin traded at $67,889.

data.title: Copper Below $13,000 on Inventory Buildup, With U.S. Rate-Cut Outlook in Focus — Market Talk
data.description:
1000 GMT - Copper prices tick lower in midmorning trade, with futures on the London Metal Exchange down 0.1% to $12,832 a metric ton. The metal is consolidating after a two-month rally fueled by speculative buying, while relatively lackluster economic data from top consumer China and a steady buildup in inventories have weighed on sentiment, according to ANZ. "Data released yesterday show readily available inventories of copper in warehouses tracked by the LME have risen for the 27th consecutive day, the longest streak since 2009," analysts at the firm say. The outlook for U.S. interest-rate cuts is also key, with traders awaiting more cues on the Federal Reserve's next move. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0956 GMT - U.K. public finances data came in better-than-expected, boosting gilts and providing relief for U.K. Treasury Chief Rachel Reeves, Pantheon Macroeconomics' Elliott Jordan-Doak says in a note. The public finances strengthened to a surplus of 30.4 billion pounds in January, from a 14.5 billion pounds surplus a year ago. The improved data is welcome ahead of the spring statement on Mar. 3, Jordan-Doak says. However, U.K. public finances could deteriorate due to rising pressure to raise government spending, he says. Yields on U.K. government bonds fall after the data, with the 10-year gilt yield last down 2.0 basis points at 4.350%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0945 GMT - Data showed better U.K. public finances in January but the improvement might not last, Capital Economics' Paul Dales says in a note. Public finances strengthened to a 30.4 billion pounds surplus in January from a 14.5 billion pounds surplus in January 2025. "Borrowing has failed to come down much this year," Dales said. The improvement isn't sustainable as data were "flattered" by capital gains tax receipts. Meanwhile, a surge in retail sales--which rose 1.8% in January from 0.4% in December--shows the economy has strengthened this year. However, the economy could struggle to grow much more than 1.0% this year amid "the lingering drag from last year's hikes in taxes for business," Dales says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0934 GMT - The euro and European government bond yields stay lower, showing limited reaction even after the eurozone's purchasing managers' index survey exceeded forecasts. The eurozone composite PMI rose to 51.9 in February from 51.3 in January, above the 51.5 expected in a WSJ survey of economists. A reading above 50 signals growth in private sector activity. It follows earlier French and German data where the two countries' composite PMIs both beat expectations. The euro falls 0.1% to $1.1760, little changed from levels before the data. Eurozone bond yields marginally trim losses, with the 10-year German Bund yield down 0.3 basis points at 2.741% and the French equivalent down 0.6 basis points at 3.311%, Tradeweb data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0929 GMT - Malaysian equities could remain attractive this year, driven by projected earnings growth of 6.4% and GDP expansion of 4.4%, CIMB Securities' Ivy Ng Lee Fang says in a note. A stronger ringgit, supportive policy measures and a potential pre-election rally could further boost sentiment, the analyst adds. She reckons domestic investors are more constructive than foreign funds, which remain cautious about earnings delivery, partly due to higher operating costs and increased taxes. Still, foreign investors have turned net buyers this year, she notes. Meanwhile, the latest earnings season has begun on a solid note, with more companies beating 4Q estimates. Ng maintains her end-2026 KLCI target of 1772. The benchmark index closed at 1752.83. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0918 GMT - Bank of Thailand is likely to cut rates at next week's meeting, according to Capital Economic's Gareth Leather in a research note. The bank is likely to deliver at least one further reduction later this year, taking the policy rate to 0.75%, says the senior Asia economist. While Thailand's economy rebounded in 4Q, further easing is needed as much of the recovery is driven by a surge in government spending, which is unlikely to be repeated, he said. The central bank is looking to "support the struggling economy, boost inflation and counter upward pressure on the baht," he said. CE notes that its forecasts are more dovish than consensus. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0900 GMT - The euro trims losses against the dollar while German government bond yields remain lower after the latest German purchasing managers' survey came in stronger than expected. The German composite purchasing managers' index rose to 53.1 in February from 52.1 in January, exceeding the 52.3 reading forecast by economists in a WSJ survey. A reading above 50 signals an expansion in private sector economic activity. It follows a slightly better-than-expected French composite print for February. The euro rises to $1.1763 after the data from $1.1755 beforehand. The 10-year German Bund yield is just under 1 basis point lower. Tradeweb data. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0855 GMT - The electrical and electronics sector, a cornerstone of Malaysia's export base, is expected to sustain its growth momentum in 2026, supported by firm global demand for semiconductor components and consumer electronics, RHB senior economist Chin Yee Sian says in a note. Structural drivers such as artificial intelligence, data centers and electric vehicles should continue to underpin semiconductor demand, she says. However, Chin flags downside risks, including weaker demand from the EU and China, shifts in U.S. trade policy, evolving regional trade dynamics and potential sector-specific tariffs, particularly on semiconductors. Still, diversified markets and trade agreements should help cushion external risks, she adds. RHB expects Malaysia to register 9.3% export growth in 2026, compared with the 6.5% recorded last year. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0844 GMT - The Chinese yuan might appreciate further, although investors remain divided on the extent, BofA Securities strategists say in a research note. Its clients remain focused on the dollar, which hit index lows in January. While Asian currencies are strengthening, investors are divided on whether the yuan's gains can beat forwards, they note. Some investors say offshore yuan proxies, such as the Malaysian ringgit and Australian dollar, might be better choices. These currencies correlate well with offshore yuan appreciation, but have higher betas and upside related to the commodity cycle and a negative correlation to dollar weakness, they note. Investors more aggressive on the yuan are looking for cheaper offshore yuan positions. BofA expects onshore yuan to trade at 6.70 against the dollar end 3Q and 4Q. The pair last traded at 6.9. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0836 GMT - Malaysia's strong January exports give the current account a solid start in 1Q, CIMB economists Chew Khai Yen and Michelle Chia say, reinforcing expectations that external demand remains a key growth driver. The upturn in electrical and electronics shipment should help buffer the economy against commodity-price swings, while non-E&E manufacturing exports remain resilient, they say. However, softer imports of intermediate and capital goods hint at some near-term cooling in domestic production. CIMB keeps its 2026 GDP growth forecast at 4.5% for now, awaiting clearer signals after the festive period. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0835 GMT - India's manufacturers and service providers are optimistic about the future, despite rising inflationary pressures, says Pranjul Bhandari, chief India economist at HSBC. The HSBC flash PMI compiled by S&P Global rose in February, remaining comfortably above the 50-mark separating expansion from contraction. The reading also indicates the strongest rate of growth for three months. The acceleration was driven by factory production, supported by robust growth in output and new domestic orders. That said, new export orders slowed, Bhandari notes. Conversely, services saw a notable acceleration in new export business, while domestic orders moderated. The PMI data sends another positive signal for India's economy at the start of 2026 as a trade deal with the U.S. clears a key overhang and new AI investments pour in. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0829 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds fall following better-than-expected public finances data. The U.K. public sector finances data for January was a surplus of 30.4 billion pounds, an improvement from the 14.5 billion surplus recorded in January 2025. The data ease concerns about the weak state of public finances, boosting gilt-market sentiment and pushing yields lower. Ten-year gilt yields fall 2.7 basis points to last trade at 4.343%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Five miners presumed dead after mudslide, South African minister says
data.description:
Five miners missing since Tuesday following a mudslide at the Ekapa diamond mine in South Africa are now presumed dead, South Africa's mining minister Gwede Mantashe said while visiting the mine, local television station eNCA reported.

The company that operates the mine, Ekapa Mining, did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

On Wednesday industry body the Minerals Council South Africa said it had deployed a senior team to try to help find the missing people.

South Africa's government and mining industry have been making efforts to reduce mine deaths and injuries, part of a campaign called "Zero Harm". Last year the country recorded its lowest-ever number of mine deaths, 41.
